data.title: Theme Switcher
content: This section is the control panel for the site's look and feel, and a sandbox where I try new UI/UX ideas. Everything here shares one architecture: a data attribute on the html element, CSS custom properties that respond to it, and localStorage so your choices survive between visits.

It exists because building is how I learn design. Ideas get tested out loud here, and I keep what works: some experiments graduate into permanent features, the rest get quietly retired. The entries after this one break down each piece that made the cut.
The same controls are also available as quick-access icons — ◐ Aa ▦ ▶ ☀ in the bottom right on desktop, or ▦ ▶ ☀ in the top right on mobile.

theme

palette

font

crt

motion

data.title: Dark / Light Mode
content: Every color on this site hangs off one attribute: data-theme on the html element. CSS custom properties define a dark and a light value for each color, so flipping that single attribute recolors the entire page at once. No per-element JavaScript, no chasing components around, and adding a new themed element costs nothing: it just references the variables and both modes work automatically.

The detail I care about most is the one you never see. On reload, an inline script in the head applies your saved theme before the first paint, so there's no flash of the wrong theme while the page loads. That flash is the tell of most dark mode implementations, and killing it is a one-script fix that has to live in exactly the right place.

data.title: Color Palette
content: Four accent palettes swap the blue, purple, and green highlights used across the site. Those three colors aren't decoration; they're the site's syntax highlighting, following the same three-part grammar as HTML markup itself. Blue marks tags — the name of a thing: a title, a technology, a specific tool. Purple marks attributes — a named property, control, or link that modifies or points to something. Green marks values — the literal data: a date, a count, a concrete fact. The convention holds everywhere from the intro to the entry you're reading now. Changing the palette reskins the syntax without breaking it.

Each palette defines separate dark and light variants, because a color that glows on a dark background can wash out completely on a light one. Picking colors you like is easy. Picking four sets of three that stay readable on two opposite backgrounds, and still feel like the same personality, is the actual design problem this feature taught me.

data.title: Font
content: Three monospace fonts, picked to sit close together instead of being as different as possible. Lekton is the site's voice by default: narrow, plain, no flourishes. Ubuntu Mono and Anonymous Pro are the alternates, chosen because their characters measure out to almost the same width as Lekton's, not just a similar look. Switching sets a single --font-mono custom property, and every text element on the site inherits the change.

That width-matching is doing real work: a noticeably wider or narrower font reflows practically everything on the page, since the whole layout is built in ch units and monospace-grid math, the animated logo included. All three fonts are self-hosted, right down to the one you're reading now, so switching never waits on a third party.

data.title: CRT Overlay
content: The full retro payoff. An html::after pseudo-element stretches across the entire viewport with pointer-events: none, so it's pure atmosphere: it tints everything and blocks nothing. The whole effect is one element, zero markup changes, and a single data attribute to switch on.

It also knows what kind of screen it's pretending to be. Dark mode renders RGB phosphor scanlines with a radial vignette — an old monitor that's been on all night. Light mode swaps to faint horizontal lines, less glowing tube, more soft static over a bright screen. Both are drawn entirely with repeating CSS gradients. No images, no canvas, no JavaScript in the render path, just math.

The overlay isn't just static, either — short glitch flashes fire on their own randomized timer the whole time it's on, like the signal itself is degrading. They're independently switchable and tunable, live, right here: turn them off entirely, or drag the slider to set the range of seconds between flashes.

glitch every
0.5s – 4.5s

data.title: Reduced Motion
content: This site likes to move: text scrambles on load, cursors blink, transitions ease. Not everyone wants that, and for some visitors motion isn't a preference but a health issue, so one toggle turns all of it off. Setting data-reduced-motion on the html element lets CSS suppress every decorative animation site-wide, from the scramble effect down to the cursor blink.

First-time visitors never have to find this toggle: the site reads your operating system's reduced-motion preference and starts there, so if you've already told your device you want less motion, this site listens. The switch below overrides it either way, and your choice is saved to localStorage, because an accessibility setting you have to re-apply every page load isn't much of a setting.

data.title: Keyboard Navigation
content: A terminal-styled site where you have to reach for the mouse would be a broken promise. The entire portfolio drives from the keyboard: number keys jump straight to any section, arrow keys walk between sections and through the items inside them, and Ctrl+K summons the command palette from anywhere.

It's not just for show, either. What started as an aesthetic commitment turned into the fastest way to use the site: once you know the shortcuts, everything is a keystroke away, and every feature I add gets wired into the same system.

1–6Jump to a section directly
← →Cycle between sections
↑ ↓Cycle through items within the current section
Ctrl+KOpen the command palette

data.title: Command Palette
content: Press Ctrl+K (Cmd+K on Mac) anywhere on the site, the same muscle memory as your editor. Start typing to search every section and item by name, and select one to jump there instantly. It's the fastest way to move around this site, and the closest thing it has to a real terminal prompt.

Matching is fuzzy: your letters just have to appear in the right order, so "crsr" still finds the Cursor Heatmap and a typo'd fragment usually lands where you meant. The matcher is hand-written like everything else here, about fifteen lines of it.

data.title: Easter Egg
content: There's a hidden trigger somewhere on this site. Find it, and it'll take you somewhere.
Hint: it's a classic.
